title: Is Nigeria’s deepwater roaring back? | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Nigeria's deepwater sector is emerging from a decade-long investment drought after Shell's 2024 Bonga North FID and 2024 fiscal incentives, with ExxonMobil, Eni and TotalEnergies advancing greenfield projects that could add 700 kb/d of liquids and 950 mmcfd of gas at peak if sanctioned within the next 18 months.

insights.claim: Nigeria's deepwater liquids output fell from a 2016 peak of around 800 kb/d to below 500 kb/d a decade later, after more than a decade without major new investment, before Bonga North reached final investment decision in late 2024.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of Nigeria's deepwater decline (over 35% drop from peak), setting the baseline against which the current recovery narrative should be measured.

insights.claim: The Nigerian government under President Bola Ahmed Tinubu is pursuing production targets of 3 million b/d and 12 bcfd of gas by 2030, using policy and regulatory reform plus fiscal incentives to reverse deepwater decline.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Sets an explicit government production target investors can use as a benchmark to track policy follow-through and Nigeria's medium-term supply trajectory.
insights.locator: What's behind Nigeria's deepwater resurgence section

insights.claim: Nigeria's 2024 fiscal incentives offer the most attractive tax credits to deepwater fields that reach final investment decision before 2029, following the 2022 extension of six legacy deepwater contracts between NNPCL and international oil companies.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Creates a hard 2029 FID deadline that should accelerate a wave of Nigerian deepwater project sanctioning in 2026-2028, a catalyst window for oilfield services and IOC capex.
insights.locator: What's behind Nigeria's deepwater resurgence section

insights.claim: Four major greenfield deepwater projects in Nigeria could combined commercialise more than 2 billion boe of resources as operators respond to the improved fiscal and regulatory environment.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A 2 billion boe resource base under active development planning represents a material reserve addition opportunity for the Majors operating in Nigeria.
insights.locator: What's behind Nigeria's deepwater resurgence section

insights.claim: Shell, ExxonMobil, Eni and TotalEnergies have high-graded their Nigerian portfolios to focus on deepwater resources that were previously economically unattractive under earlier fiscal terms, treating Nigeria as core to post-2030 portfolio priorities.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Confirms all four Majors are actively repositioning capital toward Nigerian deepwater, useful for tracking capex allocation shifts among supermajors.
insights.locator: Who are the key players section

insights.claim: Shell's 2024 FID on Bonga North, made twenty years after its discovery, was the turning point for Nigerian deepwater development, followed by ExxonMobil's approval of the US$1 billion Usan Infill Project as part of a pledged US$10 billion investment in Nigerian deepwater assets.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: ExxonMobil's $10 billion pledge is a concrete, trackable capital commitment signaling sustained multi-year Nigerian deepwater capex, not a one-off project.
insights.locator: Who are the key players section

insights.claim: TotalEnergies agreed to acquire ConOil's 50% stake in the Egina South discovery, while Shell acquired 10% of TotalEnergies' stake in OML 118 to help accelerate its Bonga Southwest-Aparo project, reflecting rising Majors' M&A activity to gain greater control over Nigerian development timelines.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Specific bilateral asset swaps/acquisitions among Majors indicate active portfolio consolidation in Nigeria, a trend investors can track for further M&A activity.
insights.locator: Who are the key players section

insights.claim: If Bonga Southwest-Aparo (Shell), Owowo (ExxonMobil), Zabazaba and Etan (Eni), Preowei, Nnwa-Doro and Bosi are developed alongside Bonga North and Usan, they could add 700 kb/d of liquids and 950 mmcfd of gas at peak to Nigerian output.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Quantifies the full potential upside case (700 kb/d liquids, 950 mmcfd gas) for Nigerian deepwater, a concrete target for investors tracking Nigeria's path to its 3 million b/d goal.
insights.quote: If developed, Bonga Southwest-Aparo, Owowo, Zabazaba, Etan, Preowei, Nnwa-Doro and Bosi – together with Bonga North and Usan – could add 700 kb/d of liquids and 950 mmcfd of gas at peak.
insights.locator: What are the key developments section

insights.claim: ExxonMobil has indicated its FID on the Bosi field will require progress on the Owowo field, while the gas-dominated Nnwa-Doro field (Shell and Chappal Energies-operated) must overcome high costs and complex ownership before proceeding.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Identifies specific inter-project dependencies and ownership complexity as near-term execution risks that could delay Nigerian deepwater FIDs.
insights.locator: What are the key developments section

insights.claim: Whether Bonga Southwest-Aparo, Zabazaba and Preowei move toward FID within the next 18 months will be the key test of whether Nigeria's deepwater resurgence has real substance, according to Wood Mackenzie's Simon Flowers.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete 18-month catalyst window for investors to monitor as confirmation (or disconfirmation) of Nigeria's deepwater recovery thesis.
insights.quote: Right now, all eyes will be on whether Bonga Southwest-Aparo, Zabazaba and Preowei move towards FID within the next 18 months. If these projects get over the line, Nigeria's deepwater resurgence will prove it has real bite.

insights.claim: Project execution remains the key risk to Nigeria's deepwater resurgence, with partner alignment, capital competition, unitisation, regulatory approvals, gas sales agreements and offshore supply-chain constraints all potentially slowing progress; projects requiring new-build FPSOs face higher costs, longer timelines and greater execution risk than tieback opportunities using spare capacity on existing FPSOs.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Gives investors a checklist of execution risk factors to screen individual Nigerian deepwater projects by, distinguishing lower-risk tiebacks from higher-risk new-build FPSO developments.
insights.locator: What are the risks to future success section
